title: Primary ciliary dyskinesia
source: Generated from structured question and KG data
vignette: A 7-year-old boy has had a chronic wet cough since infancy. He has recurrent ear infections requiring tympanostomy tubes and several episodes of sinusitis. His growth is normal, and he has no greasy stools. Chest radiograph shows bronchiectatic changes. Which underlying defect best explains his condition?
stem: Which underlying defect best explains this child's presentation?

options.text: Defective dynein arms causing impaired ciliary motility
IgA deficiency with recurrent mucosal infection

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic sinopulmonary disease beginning in infancy, recurrent otitis media, sinusitis, bronchiectasis, and preserved pancreatic function point to primary ciliary dyskinesia. The core mechanism is impaired ciliary motility from dynein arm dysfunction.
evidence: Chronic wet cough since infancy suggests lifelong mucociliary clearance failure.
Recurrent ear infections and sinusitis involve ciliated epithelium outside the lower airway.
Normal growth and absence of greasy stools make cystic fibrosis less likely.

distractors.A: CFTR dysfunction would often produce pancreatic insufficiency, poor growth, or greasy stools.
distractors.C: IgA deficiency can cause recurrent infections but does not specifically explain bronchiectasis with classic ciliary-site disease.
distractors.D: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes early emphysema or liver disease, not recurrent otitis and sinusitis from infancy.
distractors.E: Surfactant protein B deficiency usually presents as severe neonatal respiratory failure.

nodes.label: Chronic wet cough since infancy
nodes.tooltip: A lifelong wet cough suggests impaired mucociliary clearance rather than episodic asthma.

nodes.label: Recurrent ear infections
nodes.tooltip: The middle ear depends on ciliary clearance through the Eustachian tube.

nodes.label: No greasy stools
nodes.tooltip: Preserved pancreatic function helps separate PCD from cystic fibrosis.

edges.label: supports
edges.tooltip: Persistent wet cough is a clinical sign of impaired airway clearance.

edges.label: same ciliated epithelium pattern
edges.tooltip: Upper and lower airway involvement creates a multi-site ciliary pattern.

edges.label: helps exclude CF
edges.tooltip: PCD affects motile cilia, not pancreatic duct chloride transport.

title: Cystic fibrosis
source: Generated from structured question and KG data
vignette: A 5-year-old girl has recurrent pneumonia, chronic productive cough, failure to thrive, and foul-smelling greasy stools. Sweat chloride testing is elevated. Which mechanism best explains the combination of pulmonary and gastrointestinal disease?
stem: Which mechanism best explains this patient's disease?

options.text: Defective dynein arms in respiratory cilia
Immune complex deposition in small vessels
Reduced surfactant production by type II pneumocytes

explanation.whyCorrect: Cystic fibrosis is caused by CFTR dysfunction. Dehydrated airway secretions cause chronic infection and bronchiectasis, while pancreatic duct obstruction causes malabsorption, greasy stools, and poor growth.
evidence: Elevated sweat chloride is a diagnostic clue for CFTR dysfunction.
Greasy stools and failure to thrive suggest pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.
Recurrent pneumonia reflects thick mucus and chronic bacterial infection.

distractors.B: Primary ciliary dyskinesia causes impaired ciliary motility but usually spares pancreatic exocrine function.
distractors.C: Immune complex vasculitis does not explain elevated sweat chloride or pancreatic insufficiency.
distractors.D: T-cell mediated alveolar destruction is not the mechanism of cystic fibrosis.
distractors.E: Surfactant problems present in neonates rather than causing sweat chloride elevation and greasy stools.

nodes.label: Elevated sweat chloride
nodes.tooltip: High sweat chloride is the classic diagnostic marker of CFTR dysfunction.

nodes.label: Greasy stools
nodes.tooltip: Fat malabsorption implies pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.

nodes.label: Recurrent pneumonia
nodes.tooltip: Thick mucus predisposes to recurrent bacterial infection.

title: Regeneration example
source: Generated from structured question and KG data
vignette: A 10-year-old boy with known asthma develops wheezing every time he plays soccer. He uses an albuterol inhaler and improves within minutes. He has no fever and a normal chest radiograph. Which diagnosis is most likely?

explanation.whyCorrect: Symptoms triggered by exercise and relieved by albuterol support exercise-induced bronchoconstriction.
evidence: Wheezing during soccer is a trigger-linked pattern.
Rapid improvement with albuterol supports reversible bronchoconstriction.
No fever and normal chest radiograph argue against pneumonia.

distractors.B: Pneumonia would more likely include fever, focal findings, or radiographic infiltrate.
distractors.C: Foreign body aspiration usually causes sudden onset symptoms and localized findings.
distractors.D: Cystic fibrosis would be chronic with recurrent infections or malabsorption.
distractors.E: Primary ciliary dyskinesia would cause chronic wet cough, sinusitis, and otitis media.

nodes.label: Known asthma
nodes.tooltip: This makes the original item too obvious for a reasoning task.

nodes.label: Exercise trigger
nodes.tooltip: Exercise is a classic trigger for bronchoconstriction.

nodes.label: Rapid albuterol response
nodes.tooltip: Rapid bronchodilator response supports reversible airway narrowing.

regenerateTemplate.vignette: A 10-year-old boy develops cough, chest tightness, and wheezing near the end of soccer practice. Symptoms recur with intense running and improve after rest or a short-acting bronchodilator. He is afebrile, has normal oxygen saturation, and has no focal lung findings between episodes.
regenerateTemplate.stem: Which diagnosis best accounts for these recurrent exertional symptoms?

explanation.whyCorrect: The revised vignette removes the named diagnosis and makes the learner reason from exertional, reversible airway symptoms. Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction remains most likely because symptoms are reproducible with intense exercise and resolve with rest or bronchodilator therapy.
evidence: Symptoms are reproducible with intense running.
Improvement after rest or a short-acting bronchodilator supports reversibility.
Afebrile status and normal oxygen saturation make infection less likely.

distractors.B: Pneumonia is less likely without fever, persistent symptoms, or focal findings.
distractors.C: Foreign body aspiration would not recur predictably with exercise.
distractors.D: Cystic fibrosis would involve chronic respiratory and gastrointestinal features.
distractors.E: Primary ciliary dyskinesia would produce chronic wet cough and recurrent upper airway infections.
